title: Counting Rows (COUNT)
description: Quickly find out exactly how many items you have.

slides.title: How many? 🤔
slides.content:
# The big picture

Sometimes, you don't actually want to see the details of every single user. You just want to know *how many users exist in total*!

This is called an **Aggregation**: taking many rows of data, and smashing them together into one single summary number.

slides.title: The COUNT Function 🧮
slides.content:
## Counting the stars

To count the total number of rows in a table, we use the `COUNT()` function inside our SELECT statement.

`SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users;`

Instead of thousands of rows of data, the database will output exactly ONE column and ONE row: the total count (e.g., `4500`).

quizData.question: What is the primary purpose of an Aggregation function like `COUNT()`?

options.text: It returns every single row exactly as it is
It takes many rows and summarizes them into one single calculated result
It counts the amount of columns in the database

quizData.explanation: Aggregation literally means 'combining many things into a cohesive group'. COUNT takes thousands of rows and outputs a single summarized number.

slides.title: Null Values 👻
slides.content:
## The invisible data

Sometimes, a database cell is completely empty. In SQL, this is called **`NULL`**.

If you use `COUNT(*)`, it counts every row, even if everything in it is NULL. But if you count a specific column: `COUNT(email)`, it will ONLY count the rows where the `email` actually exists (ignoring the NULLs)!

quizData.question: If you have 10 users, but only 8 of them entered phone numbers, what will `COUNT(phone_number)` return?
quizData.explanation: When you specify a column inside COUNT(column_name), it meticulously ignores the rows where that column is NULL.

slides.title: Try it Out!
slides.content: There's a table named `inventory`. Find out exactly how many rows are in it, and alias it AS `total_items`.
slides.codeSnippet:
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total_items
FROM inventory;
